sentences.base_language: hello, my name is seth.
sentences.romanized: nǐ hǎo, wǒ jiào Seth.
sentences.grammarNote: **你好** (nǐ hǎo) = hello: literally 'you good.' The standard greeting. **叫** (jiào) = to be called/named. 我叫Seth = 'I am called Seth.' This is the most natural way to say your name in Chinese.

sentences.base_language: What is your name?
sentences.romanized: nǐ jiào shénme míngzi?
sentences.grammarNote: 叫 (jiào) means 'to be called'. 你叫什么名字 literally asks 'what are you called?' and is the standard way to ask someone's name.

sentences.base_language: I am from the united states.
sentences.romanized: wǒ shì měiguó rén.
sentences.grammarNote: **美国人** (měiguó rén) = American person: **美国** (America: **美** beautiful + **国** country) + **人** (person). Adding **人** to a country name gives the nationality. 中国人 (Chinese person), 法国人 (French person), 日本人 (Japanese person).

sentences.base_language: but I live in Uruguay.
sentences.romanized: dànshì wǒ zhù zài wūlāguī.
sentences.grammarNote: **住在** (zhù zài) = live at/in: **住** (zhù, to live/reside) + **在** (zài, at/in). This verb + **在** pattern places someone at a location: 住在北京 (live in Beijing), 工作在上海 (work in Shanghai). **但是** (dànshì) = but (more formal than **但**).

sentences.base_language: I'm trying to learn spanish.
sentences.romanized: wǒ zài nǔlì xué xībānyá yǔ.
sentences.grammarNote: 在 (zài) before a verb signals an ongoing action, similar to English '-ing'. 在努力学 = 'is working hard at learning'.

sentences.base_language: Do you want to learn with me?
sentences.romanized: nǐ xiǎng hé wǒ yīqǐ xué ma?
sentences.grammarNote: **和** (hé) = and/with. **一起** (yīqǐ) = together — always comes before the verb: 和我一起学 = 'learn together with me.' **想** (xiǎng) = want to / would like to (softer than **要**). The particle **吗** at the end makes it a yes/no question.

sentences.base_language: My girlfriend is named Kayla.
sentences.romanized: wǒ nǚpéngyou jiào Kayla.
sentences.grammarNote: **女朋友** (nǚpéngyou) = girlfriend: **女** (nǚ, female) + **朋友** (friend). **男朋友** = boyfriend. Note: no **的** is needed here — possessive with personal pronouns before close relationships is often dropped: 我女朋友 not 我的女朋友 (though both are fine).

sentences.base_language: She is a nice woman.
sentences.romanized: tā shì yī gè hěn hǎo de nǚrén.
sentences.grammarNote: **个** (gè) is the general-purpose measure word used for people when no more specific measure word applies. **很好的** uses **的** (de) to link the adjective phrase to the noun: adjective + **的** + noun. This **的** is the possessive/descriptive particle.

sentences.base_language: We like to go on walks.
sentences.romanized: wǒmen xǐhuān sànbù.
sentences.grammarNote: **喜欢** (xǐhuān) = to like. In Chinese, **喜欢** is followed directly by a verb or noun with no 'to' equivalent: 喜欢散步 = 'like walking.' **散步** (sànbù) = to take a stroll: **散** (sàn, scatter/leisurely) + **步** (bù, step).

sentences.base_language: We like to cook together.
sentences.romanized: wǒmen xǐhuān yīqǐ zuòfàn.
sentences.grammarNote: **做饭** (zuòfàn) = to cook: **做** (zuò, to make/do) + **饭** (fàn, cooked rice/food). Even though 饭 literally means 'rice,' 做饭 means cooking in general. **一起** (yīqǐ) = together — placed before the verb.

sentences.base_language: She is a really good chef.
sentences.romanized: tā shì yī gè hěn hǎo de chúshī.
sentences.grammarNote: **厨师** (chúshī) = chef: **厨** (chú, kitchen) + **师** (shī, master/teacher). **师** is a suffix for skilled professionals: 老师 (teacher), 医师 (physician), 工程师 (engineer). The **是...一个...** pattern is the standard way to say someone is 'a [profession].'

sentences.base_language: I am not a great chef but I have a secret trick.
sentences.target_language: 我不是一个好厨师，但我有一个秘密技巧。
sentences.romanized: wǒ bú shì yī gè hǎo chúshī, dàn wǒ yǒu yī gè mìmì jìqiǎo.
sentences.grammarNote: **不是** (bú shì) = am not / is not — the negation of **是** (to be). Note the tone change: **不** is 4th tone but becomes 2nd tone (bú) before another 4th-tone syllable. **秘密** (mìmì) = secret. **技巧** (jìqiǎo) = skill/technique: **技** (skill) + **巧** (clever/dexterous).

sentences.base_language: I use a lot of Siracha sauce!
sentences.romanized: wǒ yòng hěn duō shìlāchā jiàng!
sentences.grammarNote: **用** (yòng) = to use. **很多** (hěn duō) = a lot / many: **很** (very) + **多** (many/much). **酱** (jiàng) = sauce/paste — found in many food words: 番茄酱 (ketchup), 豆瓣酱 (chili bean paste), 酱油 (soy sauce).

sentences.base_language: My best friend is Jeff.
sentences.romanized: wǒ zuì hǎo de péngyou shì Jeff.
sentences.grammarNote: **最** (zuì) = most — the superlative marker placed before the adjective: 最好 (best), 最大 (biggest), 最喜欢 (like most). **最好的朋友** = best friend. The **的** links the superlative phrase to the noun **朋友**.

sentences.base_language: Yes, the jeff that made this app.
sentences.target_language: 是的，就是做这个应用的Jeff。
sentences.romanized: shì de, jiù shì zuò zhège yìngyòng de Jeff.
sentences.grammarNote: **就是** (jiù shì) = exactly is / none other than — used for emphasis and identification. **做这个应用的Jeff** uses a relative clause before the noun: verb phrase + **的** + noun = 'the Jeff who made this app.' Chinese relative clauses always come BEFORE the noun they modify.

sentences.base_language: He works as a programmer.
sentences.romanized: tā shì yī gè chéngxùyuán.
sentences.grammarNote: **程序员** (chéngxùyuán) = programmer: **程序** (chéngxù, program/procedure) + **员** (yuán, person/member). **员** is a common suffix for occupations: 服务员 (waiter), 售货员 (salesperson), 运动员 (athlete).

sentences.base_language: I have many other friends too.
sentences.romanized: wǒ hái yǒu hěn duō qítā péngyou.
sentences.grammarNote: **还** (hái) = also / still / in addition. When placed before **有**, it means 'also have / there are more.' **其他** (qítā) = other. Note: **朋友** has no plural marker — Chinese nouns don't change form for plural; context and numbers make plurality clear.

sentences.base_language: Stephen and Leslie are very nice.
sentences.romanized: Stephen hé Leslie rén hěn hǎo.
sentences.grammarNote: The phrase **人很好** (rén hěn hǎo) literally means 'person very good' — a set expression meaning 'is a very nice person / is very kind.' Chinese often drops the subject pronoun in predicate comments: [name] + 人很好 = '[name] is a really nice person.'

sentences.base_language: They do a lot of hobbies.
sentences.romanized: tāmen yǒu hěn duō àihào.
sentences.grammarNote: **爱好** (àihào) = hobby/interest: **爱** (love) + **好** (like). In Chinese you 'have' hobbies (有爱好) rather than 'do' them. **有很多爱好** = 'have many hobbies.' The verb **有** is used for possession and existence.

sentences.base_language: Leslie can make really pretty costumes.
sentences.romanized: Leslie néng zuò hěn piàoliang de fúzhuāng.
sentences.grammarNote: **能** (néng) = can / be able to (physical or circumstantial ability). Compare: **会** (huì) = can (learned skill), **可以** (kěyǐ) = may/can (permission). **漂亮** (piàoliang) = pretty/beautiful — a common adjective that loses its tones in casual speech (liang becomes neutral).

sentences.base_language: Stephen likes music festivals.
sentences.romanized: Stephen xǐhuān yīnyuè jié.
sentences.grammarNote: **音乐节** (yīnyuè jié) = music festival: **音乐** (yīnyuè, music: **音** sound + **乐** music) + **节** (jié, festival/holiday). **节** is used for festivals: 春节 (Spring Festival), 节日 (holiday). Compare 音乐会 (yīnyuèhuì) = concert (smaller, ticketed event).

sentences.base_language: Together they are a lot of fun.
sentences.romanized: tāmen zài yīqǐ hěn yǒuqù.
sentences.grammarNote: 有趣 (yǒuqù) literally means 'have interest/fun'. It's a very common adjective for 'interesting' or 'fun'.

sentences.base_language: What are your friends like? Do you have a boyfriend or girlfriend?
sentences.target_language: 你的朋友们怎么样？你有男朋友或女朋友吗？
sentences.romanized: nǐ de péngyoumen zěnmeyàng? nǐ yǒu nánpéngyou huò nǚpéngyou ma?
sentences.grammarNote: **们** (men) is the plural suffix for pronouns and people: 朋友们 (friends), 我们 (we), 你们 (you all). **怎么样** (zěnmeyàng) = how is it? / what's it like? **或** (huò) = or (used in statements/questions). Compare **还是** (háishì) = or (used in choice questions between two options).
